speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are finally in Detroit, living on Burlingame with Uncle Max and Bertha. Naturally, we're introduced to their children, my cousins. My cousin Herman was born when my father was born. And he came here just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before World War I or after World War I, so they knew each other. Naturally, was changed and met. For the first time, I met my cousin Moe Fuman, Uncle Max's and Bertha's son. Now, he was a college graduate already then. Here I could hardly manage my English. First thing they did, they have, you know, sent us to Americanization class to learn the language. We went to Northern High School. We just take the bus and the streetcar, which, naturally, Uncle Max provided the money. And we learned English, civics, and little things like, but mostly social things at school, maybe not how to eat or drink, but about the United States. And our class was United Nations class, different. My brother who was younger, went to another class and I was in this class. And we had people from Greece and from you name it, from Europe. There were two young men of Greek origin who were born in the United States in Detroit. Before the war, they went to Greece. They could never come back during the war, so naturally, they came. They claimed US citizenship, but they lost their language. They lost English, so they had to learn English. But we made good progress. We had to write stories and come in front of the class and deliver a speech, you know, to get along with the language, to master the language. There was one young man from Greece who used to like to go up front of this class and tell stories. And one day he was telling about this problem in this story. He kept using the word troublous. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And over, troublous here, troublous there. All kinds of little incidents with the word troublous. No one could figure out what is troublous. And he went up to the blackboard and he wrote it out, troubles, T-R-O-U-B-L-E-S. That's-- you pronounce it, you know, the way it's written, by sound, but not the English way, troubles. It's troublous. That was very, very cute. We had such a terrific laugh. That's what his nickname became, Troublous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first summer. Then I was sent to high school, so I continued going to Northern High School, which was, I didn't know how about it. It was out of my district.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, naturally, I had a very difficult time. And it was mixed already. Didn't make any difference. A student is a student. But let me tell you that I did not-- I looked at myself now-- that I did not react-- I'm not sure how to say-- I had no big reaction because I was in a school with blacks and whites, or Afro Americans. Because we were in Germany, when we crossed the border, we were there for a week in a camp called Hof. It was at the Czech-German border and there was a big United States Army camp, installation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to see these soldiers walking back and forth all over by the gates. Must have been a black division, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, an American blacks division. This was the first time in my life that I saw an Afro American. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's fascinating. You know, they said black people, but how can you imagine what they look like? You know? This was my first encounter. So I used to go to the fence and look at, them you know, to see what they're like, the same way people looked at me in Michigan, Traverse City area. I was in the United States Army Reserves. I was actually attached to the National Guard during the Korean War because I knew Russian, so I was put into an intelligence unit. We were just interpreters. And we used to go to Grayling for two weeks summer training, but in the evening, we would go. So we went to Traverse City to the bars looking for adventures like any red-blooded American does, and we met up with some girls there. And I was one of the Jews. And there were three other-- no, two others. And there were seven of us total, the three Jews and the four Christians. We were all in the same unit, same one unit intelligence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, naturally, we haven't got time. We're trying to make count. You know how things go. And one of the girls said, oh, you're from Detroit. She tells me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, I heard that there's a lot of Jews in Detroit. Yes. Aren't you afraid? No. Oh, she says, I wouldn't live there. They're terrible. What do you mean terrible? Come on, they're just like any other people. Oh, she said, they come here and all kinds of funny stories, scary stories about Jews. So, you know, I finally got up and I go, well, it's a lost evening. So I said to her, how did you like dancing with Steve? Steve Radoff. He's a good dancer. OK. And his cousin-- I don't remember what his cousin's name was. How was he? He's nice. So how did you like the stories and the joking and the beer and all the entertainment here? Oh, it's very nice. I'm going to tell you a secret. I'm Jewish, Steve is Jewish, and his cousin is Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she said, can't be. Ask, you know, the other guys. And the other guys confirmed. She was flabbergasted. She just could not understand that it's possible. So I guess maybe I had the same impression about blacks at that time. I thought that they'll be different. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Northern High School for a year. I was transferred, then I was told I have to attend Central High School. Central High School at that time, this was '48. I found out later it's a top-notch school. They send a lot of their students to college, whatever that means, to the university. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: With great difficulty, I graduated Central High School. You know, the language is a big barrier. Not only they have to learn to pronounce it, you have to know how to spell it. You have to put thoughts in English. And at that time, I had difficulty, you know, thinking in the language that you're learning. Then after school, I used to sit in the basement and read books with dictionaries. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tomorrow, I wanted to know good English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the word parentheses, what's a parentheses? Are you taking algebra? What's a parentheses? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have to answer the teacher night away. She doesn't know you've just got off the boat. It was great difficulty.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my fellow students in high school introduced me to a Jewish young people's group, young men, teenagers in high school, B'nai B'rith Youth Group. We are still members. We still meet up. Now, we don't meet like we used to because we are scattered all over the country, but those that are here, the first Wednesday of the month, we go out to dinner, talk stories, have a drink. We drink whiskey, beer, whatever want to first.

speaker: interviewer
text: After high school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I want to say is that the youth group was a big, big thing for me. Why? Because I found out how things work, that you can go to university here and it's easy to get in as long as you have the grades with no problems. A Jewish guy can go to university. Wow, that's amazing. Well, some of them graduated before me, some graduated after me and I'm in the middle. I graduated with some of them. And I came home after graduation with my cap and gown. Well, what are you going to do? I said I would like to go to university like my friends. And I think my father almost fainted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen, he's only been a year in the country. After two years, he was in hard financial straights because he was paying his sister back, not so much his sister as his brother-in-law for the money that he advanced to bring us over here. You've got to pay your debts. And he found I got to go to university, you gotta pay money. That costs money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, you don't have to go to college. There's plenty of work without college in the United States. But I kept sticking to my guns. I'm going to go. So I went to Wayne State University. Naturally, I have to take an entrance exam because I didn't have A's and B's. And I took the exam. The man called me up and he came to confidence. You failed the English. This is Korean War started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, you know, I'm going to be drafted in the army. He said, it's OK. That's where you're going to learn the language. I said, Mr. Tislack, I thought to myself, I think that in the Army the only thing you learn is excellent way of swearing. I told them that and he thought come back when you're through with the Army. I said, oh, no. I'm going to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I work hard. I got a little bit defeated. Can't get in. I failed English. What am I going to do? And I go to the corner to take the bus. One of my friends from the B'nai B'rith Group, George Dean, is on the corner. We start talking. I tell him my problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, Marten, you can do it. He says, he made it. I can do it. I should know. George never worked after school in high school and I worked every day. And it's difficult. You have to go to school, you have to socialize, and work too. So it was very hard, but he says, he put this spirit into me and I said, if George says so, it's got to be. So I made it. I went part time and night time and day time. Did OK. I survived. The first year was very bad because I had to take English and I had a class with war veterans. And some of them were officers in the Army. And I was devastated by the English. It's difficult. And the teacher told me, he says, you better drop out because you're not going to survive. Your caliber's-- you know, your English and their English are two worlds apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started at the bottom. English for foreigners. So I made it. And then came the liberal arts business. Have to make up your mind and this career stuff. Well, I graduated. I made it, got a degree in liberal arts, and before I got the liberal arts, I think I met up with Florence and naturally, we got married. What comes first? It was difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a job, which was a big struggle because we had a kid. Miles was born. He was born. Sorry he's not here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get an advanced degree, Marten?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not get an advanced degree. At the place where I work, Miss Stout start challenging me. What am I doing there? I worked for the Welfare Department for the State. She challenged me and she brought me an article about pharmacy. I'm not going to go to pharmacy. You know, you're always cooped up behind the counter over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I paid attention. I visited some of my friends. Guess what? I enrolled in pharmacy school. They told me after three years to go. That was murder.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have two children. Florence went to work. We have to have a babysitter and I got to go to school full time. Listen, I used to get up six in the morning to be at school at eight. After school, I went to work because we couldn't survive and I need the money that last. Then I went to summer school. One summer I took chemistry at the University of Detroit and I was ready to quit. It was horrendous. You spend six, seven hours a day in a chemistry lab and you're exhausted. You know, hot, no air conditioning. You're washing, you're weighing, you're calculating, you weigh and you wash. But I kept hoping this summer, one more year, maybe easier. No, it was not easy. And I made it. Let me tell you, when I finally got my license, Mr. Samuel Golder told me hang it up, hurry up. We had a favorite frame, fancy. I'm not going to spend $15 on a fancy frame. I went to Kmart and I bought a frame and it's lasted me all this time. It was very nice. I was able to provide very nicely for my family. I have a very lovely wife. She's very nice to me and she raised very nice children. Because I didn't know about raising children and educating. I knew how to provide for them, but children need more than just providing. And the children are very nice to me. In fact, Glenda came. When she found out. I'm having this taping, she came on her own with her husband. He's a very nice young man. My son who's a doctor now, he couldn't make it because all this scheduling over there, his wife and two little sons, grandsons, but he's one hell of a guy. He became a dentist. He was a dentist two years and he didn't like it and he decided to go to med school. He had all our support. It's a very hard job to become a physician. Let me tell you it's a lot of work. Not only is it a lot of work, but you have to have the brains for it. I could have never made it. That's why I'm not. He's a bright young man. And our girls are bright young ladies. I'm never ashamed of them, always proud of them. More kids like that would be fine. The world would be much nicer. They've always paid attention to us. They always obeyed us. They understood what's involved. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They all four went to Yovam. Three graduated from there, Actually, Miles did not graduate from Yovam. I'm sorry. But he went after his third year at Yovam was accepted to Dental School at U of D. Glenda got a master's degree in public health from Yuvam. Arlene graduated in business administration from Yovam. Pamela went for three years to Yovam.. I guess it was too hard for her, but she finished at Eastern. They're nice kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, if there was a statement that you would like to make to the world, do you have any that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. The only thing I want to say is I wish people would be more tolerant and understanding with each other and the rest will take care of itself. I would like to thank all the nice Christian people of the village of Hruszwica [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] for making it possible for us to survive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would like to thank very much the late Mr. Stakh Denishchuk. I would like to thank his son, Sergei Denishchuk, whom I wrote a letter in '91 and invited him to come here, but has told me that due to his health, he could not make it. It would have been a great pleasure to have him here. But instead his son and his daughter and his granddaughter came. His son was a very disappointment to me. But yet, in their honor, Stakh and Sergei very nice people. People with compassion. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank Vasilii Daniluk who was such a terror, or we thought he was such a terror, but so nice. I never told anybody that when he saw us, that we were around somewhere in the village. Maybe his grandchildren will see this program. Maybe they'll read about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thank their grandfather for it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to thank [? Chosna ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and Mrs. [? Chosna, ?] although they're both dead. Every so often I send money to put a bunch of flowers on their grave. It's the least I can do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Mr. Zumer deserves a very nice thank you also. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For all the rumors about him and for all the things he did, he did a nice thing for us. Mr. Dvorak from Martynowka. I hope his descendants will regard him as a kind man although sometimes I thought he was a holy terror. And he loved fish, especially gefilte fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: [YIDDISH] my aunt Chaika sprang a very big surprise on him once. She was with us in the haystack on his farm. And she cooked fried fish. And they were sitting around and talking. And he says to Aunt Chaika, he says, and where were-- he asked her, where you hidden during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told him, in your haystack. He was flabbergasted because he didn't know she was there. He was very much surprised. I hope they rest in peace. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And may their descendants have peace too. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I would like to offer this little prayer, little prayer from a prayer book. 'Blessed art thou, the Lord our God, King of the universe, who brought miracles. [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [HEBREW] Let us live to these times.'

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, this last week [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was very stressful. I thought and re-thought how should I say my story. How should explain the cruelty and the humanity that were brought upon a group of people who have behaved like human beings in the village and yet by their very neighbors were so decimated? A little girl of four, a little girl of six or seven, the old, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the women. And I regard women much nicer than men in their behavior to humanity, yet they met such an ugly death. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult not to cry or not to feel sad and feel angry when people go out in the public and deny that such a thing happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yet my neighbors from the Ukraine, from [INAUDIBLE] who I correspond with, acknowledge in their letters what a difficult time we had. They had a difficult time too. I don't deny that. But they were not annihilated as a people. If Olga lost a brother, her mother survived, her sister survived, her uncle survived, her grandparents survived. In my case, if it wasn't for their generosity, I wouldn't be here. My grandma with her grandchildren, as with her daughter, did not deserve to die a death like that, to be smashed by a gun or be smashed by a two by four. And then their teeth pulled out just because they had dental works. And who knows what else they were robbed of. Yet they lie in an unmarked grave among bushes where animals tread. Not right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We all people have the same religious root, yet some of us disregard it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not fair. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] I would like you to see my children. The three girls are here. My brother is here and my brother still has problems about this nightmare. I have bad dreams once in a while. But every so often, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not much you can do. But most of all, I'm very happy to be here in this country, among these people and enjoy such nice freedoms. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Freedoms come in various shapes and very generous freedom. I hope it stays like this for a long time so other people and our children's children enjoy it too. Thank you.
